content:
The Moment I Realized I Was Using Claude Code Wrong
For months, my workflow looked the same.
Open Claude Code.
Paste code.
Ask a question.
Wait for an answer.
Repeat.
At first, it felt incredibly productive.
But as my projects became larger, things started falling apart.
Long conversations became difficult to manage.
Claude began forgetting earlier parts of the discussion.
I found myself opening multiple chat sessions just to keep context organized.
Sometimes I’d spend more time explaining my project again than actually building it.
I assumed this was just how AI coding assistants worked.
I couldn’t have been more wrong.
One afternoon, while watching another developer’s workflow, I noticed something unusual.
Instead of typing another prompt, they entered a command beginning with a forward slash.
A few seconds later, Claude reorganized the conversation instantly.
Then they used another command.
And another.
Within minutes, they were navigating files, managing context, clearing conversations, and switching tasks far faster than I ever could.
That’s when I realized I’d been using perhaps 10% of Claude Code’s capabilities.
The biggest productivity boost wasn’t learning better prompts.
It was learning the commands hiding in plain sight.

My Biggest Takeaway
The biggest lesson wasn’t learning fourteen commands.
It was realizing that powerful tools only become powerful when you learn how they’re designed to be used.
For months I blamed AI whenever my workflow became slow.
In reality, I simply hadn’t learned the tool well enough.
Now my conversations stay organized.
Debugging feels faster.
Large projects are easier to manage.
And I spend far less time repeating myself.

Final Thoughts
AI coding assistants are evolving incredibly fast.
The developers who get the most value aren’t necessarily the ones writing the best prompts.
They’re the ones who understand the entire workflow.
If you’re using Claude Code every day, take an hour to explore its built-in commands.
That small investment can save you countless hours over the coming months.
Sometimes the biggest productivity upgrade isn’t a new AI model.
It’s discovering features that have been there all along.
